title: The Blackcomb question: can the pipeline reach its full 2.5 bcfd capacity at startup?  | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Wood Mackenzie's mid-May 2026 aerial surveillance estimates Whitewater's 366-mile Blackcomb Pipeline (Waha to Agua Dulce) at 63% mainline complete, with a Q4 2026 startup achievable but a phased ramp likely: initial capacity of ~2 bcfd versus 2.5 bcfd design, gated by lagging compression build-out and the badly-behind Big Wells station.

insights.claim: Whitewater's Blackcomb Pipeline is expected to enter service at roughly 2 bcfd of initial operational capacity, short of its 2.5 bcfd design throughput, with the remaining ~0.5 bcfd unlocked only as later compression stages finish in the months after first gas.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: Shippers and marketers modeling Permian takeaway relief should base-case ~2 bcfd, not 2.5 bcfd, at launch and price the ~0.5 bcfd shortfall until the compression build-out closes.
insights.quote: Wood Mackenzie expects Blackcomb to enter service at an initial operational capacity of approximately 2 bcfd, falling short of its 2.5 bcfd design throughput.
insights.locator: On that basis, Wood Mackenzie expects

insights.claim: A Q4 2026 in-service date for Blackcomb remains achievable, but Wood Mackenzie views a phased capacity startup as the more probable outcome rather than a full-capacity launch.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: Traders should treat a full-capacity Q4 2026 debut as a low-probability scenario and plan basis exposure around a staged ramp.
insights.quote: A Q4 2026 in-service date remains achievable, but we view a phased capacity startup as the more probable outcome given the compression picture.

insights.claim: Blackcomb's likely 2 bcfd initial launch mirrors the constrained first-phase, four-compressor-station capacity of Whitewater's sister Whistler Pipeline, suggesting a repeated operator playbook of bringing mainline online ahead of full compression.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: Use the Whistler precedent as a template for handicapping Blackcomb's ramp cadence and any future Whitewater Permian pipeline startups.
insights.quote: similar to the initial four compressor station capacity availabile on Whitewater’s sister Whistler Pipeline project
insights.locator: Future compression sites identified near Ozona and Uvalde

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's flight imagery estimates Blackcomb's 366-mile mainline (Waha hub to Agua Dulce, Texas) at 63% complete as of the mid-May 2026 flyover.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: A hard 63% completion mark gives an independent, filings-beating checkpoint to validate whether the Q4 timeline is on track.
insights.quote: Our flight imagery review estimates Blackcomb's mainline at 63% complete.

insights.claim: Pipe installation on Blackcomb's Permian and Southern spreads is well in hand, leaving the Central corridor through Val Verde and Kinney counties as the active construction front and the key schedule variable for the expected Q4 startup.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: Monitor the Central corridor specifically, not overall project optics, as the true gate on in-service timing.
insights.quote: The remaining mainline challenge is concentrated in the Central corridor through Val Verde and Kinney counties, now the active construction front and the key schedule variable for an expected Q4 startup.
insights.locator: The 366-mile route connecting the Waha hub to Agua Dulce

themes.theme: Compression Readiness and Big Wells Risk

insights.claim: The Big Wells Compressor Station in Dimmit County showed only preliminary grading as of the May 2026 flyover, a structural lag versus every other facility on Blackcomb and the primary risk that could constrain initial flow capacity.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: Big Wells is the single asset to watch; if it isn't advanced by the time the mainline wraps, the 2 bcfd cap holds and possibly worsens.
insights.quote: The structural lag at Big Wells is the primary risk to watch.
insights.locator: The structural lag at Big Wells

insights.claim: Compression readiness across Blackcomb varies widely: Rankin is in late-stage construction near mechanical completion, Del Rio is in solid mid-stage execution, and Freer, only recently filed with the TCEQ, already shows poured concrete foundations.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: Freer's fast-tracked start signals the operator is proactively front-loading compression, partially offsetting the Big Wells drag.
insights.quote: a site only recently filed with the TCEQ, which is already showing poured concrete foundations, suggesting the operator is proactively managing the compression schedule

insights.claim: Closing Blackcomb's gap from ~2 bcfd to 2.5 bcfd design throughput hinges on completing outstanding compression, including future sites identified near Ozona and Uvalde, in the months following first gas.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: Track Ozona and Uvalde compression progress as the leading indicators for when full 2.5 bcfd capacity actually arrives.
insights.quote: Closing that gap will require completion of the outstanding compression build-out, including sites near Ozona and Uvalde, in the months following first gas.
insights.locator: On that basis, Wood Mackenzie expects

insights.claim: A constrained Blackcomb initial ramp is a meaningful commercial consideration for shippers and counterparties planning around the pipeline's volumes, since delayed compression directly limits deliverable capacity.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: Counterparties relying on Blackcomb volumes should hedge for a slower-than-nameplate ramp in their supply and offtake planning.
insights.quote: initial flow capacity will be constrained, a meaningful commercial consideration for shippers and counterparties planning around Blackcomb's volumes
insights.locator: The structural lag at Big Wells

insights.claim: The timing and sequencing of Blackcomb's capacity ramp matters directly for producers and marketers exposed to the Permian takeaway market.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: Waha basis relief tied to Blackcomb should be discounted for a phased ramp, keeping Permian gas price risk elevated longer than a full-capacity launch would imply.
insights.quote: For producers and marketers exposed to the Permian takeaway market, the timing and sequencing of that ramp matters.

insights.claim: Pipeline projects influence markets long before they enter service, with construction progress, compression build-out and commissioning timelines all affecting the pace at which new capacity reaches the system.
insights.speaker: Devin Cao
insights.so_what: Watch proprietary aerial-surveillance signals ahead of public filings to front-run capacity-driven basis moves.
insights.quote: Pipeline projects influence markets long before they enter service.
